title: The mTOR–autophagy axis: why fasting works and how to time it right
excerpt: A deep look at the cellular mechanisms behind caloric restriction, the evidence on time-restricted eating windows, and what the latest trials actually show about longevity outcomes.

content.text: The mTOR pathway is the master regulator of cellular growth. When you eat, mTOR is active — cells grow, protein synthesis runs, anabolism dominates. When you fast, mTOR quiets, and autophagy — the cellular cleanup mechanism — takes over. This switch is the mechanistic basis for most of the benefits attributed to caloric restriction.

mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin) integrates signals from nutrients, growth factors, and energy status. It exists in two complexes: mTORC1 — the main growth regulator — and mTORC2, which handles metabolism and cytoskeletal organization. When you eat protein, especially leucine-rich sources, mTORC1 activates within minutes. When you fast, nutrient signals drop, AMPK rises, and mTORC1 is suppressed.

This suppression is the trigger for autophagy. Without mTOR pressing the accelerator, the cell shifts resources toward maintenance: degrading damaged proteins, clearing dysfunctional organelles, and recycling cellular components. The Yoshinori Ohsumi Nobel Prize (2016) formalized the molecular machinery — ATG genes orchestrating the formation of autophagosomes that engulf cellular debris and deliver it to lysosomes for breakdown.
Autophagy is not just cellular recycling. It is the mechanism by which cells remove damaged components before they accumulate into the pathology we call ageing.
The fasting window: how long is enough?

mTOR suppression sufficient to initiate autophagy appears to require at minimum 12–14 hours of fasting in humans. The commonly cited 16:8 window is conservative but well-supported. Protein intake — particularly leucine — is among the most potent mTOR activators, which explains why even a small protein-containing snack can reset the autophagic window.

Carbohydrates and fat have weaker mTOR-activating effects than protein, though they still raise insulin and suppress autophagy to a degree. Black coffee and water do not appear to significantly impact the autophagic window — a commonly debated point that the literature generally supports.
What the human evidence actually shows

The longevity evidence in humans remains observational or short-term mechanistic. No randomized controlled trial has followed humans through decades of fasting to measure mortality outcomes. What we have: consistently upregulated autophagy markers (beclin-1, LC3-II) after 24h fasts; reduced insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) with prolonged caloric restriction; and epidemiological data linking time-restricted eating to reduced metabolic disease risk.
Levine B, Kroemer G. (2008). Autophagy in the pathogenesis of disease. Cell 132(1):27-42.
Longo VD, Panda S. (2016). Fasting, circadian rhythms, and time-restricted feeding in healthy lifespan. Cell Metab 23(6):1048-1059.
Anton SD et al. (2018). Flipping the metabolic switch: understanding and applying the health benefits of fasting. Obesity 26(2):254-268.

Based on the mechanistic literature, a 16:8 window (16h fast, 8h eating) appears sufficient for regular autophagy induction. Breaking the fast with protein-rich food maximises the anabolic window. For those interested in deeper autophagy induction, 24h fasts once or twice weekly have the strongest evidence base — but add meaningful compliance difficulty.
